title: Clone and remote configuration

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Clone and remote configuration; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. sources.claim: Clone creates a local repository, copies reachable objects and remote configuration, creates remote-tracking references, and checks out an initial working branch.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Clone creates a local repository, copies reachable objects and remote configuration, creates remote-tracking references, and checks out an initial working branch.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Cloning a public repository creates local origin configuration and an origin/main tracking reference while local changes remain entirely local. Your goal is to clone a sandbox repository and explain each local and remote-tracking ref. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Branches, merges and conflicts; Branches as movable refs. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Clone creates a local repository, copies reachable objects and remote configuration, creates remote-tracking references, and checks out an initial working branch. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Authentication, partial or shallow clone, submodules, large files, default-branch choice, and server permissions can change what is fetched or checked out. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Clone creates a live shared folder where local edits immediately alter the remote repository.

includes: Create a local repository from a remote and inspect names, URLs and default branches.
Clone a sandbox repository and explain each local and remote-tracking ref.
excludes: Authentication, partial or shallow clone, submodules, large files, default-branch choice, and server permissions can change what is fetched or checked out.

outcomes: Clone a sandbox repository and explain each local and remote-tracking ref.

examples.title: Clone and remote configuration: direct case
examples.situation: Cloning a public repository creates local origin configuration and an origin/main tracking reference while local changes remain entirely local.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: Cloning a public repository creates local origin configuration and an origin/main tracking reference while local changes remain entirely local.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: A clone is an independent local repository initialized from remote data; later fetch and push operations synchronize selected references explicitly.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because A clone is an independent local repository initialized from remote data; later fetch and push operations synchronize selected references explicitly.

examples.title: Clone and remote configuration: boundary case
examples.situation: A shallow clone contains limited ancestry, so a history query cannot assume every older commit is available locally.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A shallow clone contains limited ancestry, so a history query cannot assume every older commit is available locally.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Authentication, partial or shallow clone, submodules, large files, default-branch choice, and server permissions can change what is fetched or checked out.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Clone creates a live shared folder where local edits immediately alter the remote repository.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Clone a sandbox repository and explain each local and remote-tracking ref.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Clone creates a live shared folder where local edits immediately alter the remote repository.
misconceptions.correction: A clone is an independent local repository initialized from remote data; later fetch and push operations synchronize selected references explicitly.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Clone creates a live shared folder where local edits immediately alter the remote repository.

analogy.example: Two libraries exchanging catalog updates can model Clone and remote configuration: each keeps an independent collection and synchronizes selected records deliberately.
usefulFor: Separating local history, remote observations, download, integration, and publication
limitations: Git refspecs, authentication, ancestry checks, force updates, and object transfer have no exact library equivalent.
Authentication, partial or shallow clone, submodules, large files, default-branch choice, and server permissions can change what is fetched or checked out.

recap.summary: You should now be able to clone a sandbox repository and explain each local and remote-tracking ref. Keep this boundary in view: Authentication, partial or shallow clone, submodules, large files, default-branch choice, and server permissions can change what is fetched or checked out.
